title: Why an Emergency Fund Comes First
summary: Before investing or upgrading your lifestyle, set aside 3-6 months of essential expenses in a safe, easy-to-reach account. It absorbs shocks like a job loss or medical bill without forcing you into debt.

sections.heading: What it is
sections.body: An emergency fund is cash kept aside only for genuine emergencies — not holidays, not gadgets. It lives somewhere safe and liquid, like a savings account or a liquid fund.

sections.heading: How big should it be?
sections.body: A common starting target is 3 months of essential expenses, building toward 6 months. Higher if your income is irregular (freelance) or you support family.

sections.heading: Why it comes before investing
sections.body: Without a buffer, one surprise bill can force you to sell investments at a bad time or borrow at high interest. The fund is what lets your long-term money stay invested.

quiz.prompt: An emergency fund is best kept in:
options: A volatile stock you can sell anytime
A safe, liquid account you can access quickly

quiz.prompt: A reasonable starting target for an emergency fund is:
Who should usually keep a larger emergency fund (closer to 6 months)?
options: Someone with a stable salaried job and no dependents
Someone with irregular freelance income or family to support
Someone who already has lots of credit cards

quiz.prompt: An emergency fund is meant to be used for:
options: A planned holiday or a new gadget
A genuine emergency like a job loss or medical bill
Buying more stocks when markets fall

quiz.prompt: Why does an emergency fund come before investing?
options: It lets your long-term money stay invested instead of being sold at a bad time
It earns higher returns than any investment
Investing is not allowed without one

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
